quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Exponential growth/decay DE"?

options.text: [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a); the average rate of change over [a,b]; the slope of the line connecting endpoints
dy/dt = ky has solution y(t) = y₀eᵏᵗ; k > 0 = growth, k < 0 = decay
A function can have more than one point satisfying MVT; each is where the tangent is parallel to the secant
Sometimes algebraic simplification (canceling, expanding, dividing) before differentiation is easier than applying rules to complex expressions

quiz_questions.explanation: "Exponential growth/decay DE" is defined as: dy/dt = ky has solution y(t) = y₀eᵏᵗ; k > 0 = growth, k < 0 = decay. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Doubling time"?

options.text: t₂ = ln(2)/k; the time for the quantity to double (when k > 0)
f''(x) > 0; the graph curves upward like a cup; the slope is increasing
Left-hand limit: lim(x→c⁻) f(x); right-hand limit: lim(x→c⁺) f(x); the two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal
∫f'(x)/f(x)dx = ln|f(x)| + C (u = f(x)); ∫f'(x)·[f(x)]ⁿdx = [f(x)]ⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C

quiz_questions.explanation: "Doubling time" is defined as: t₂ = ln(2)/k; the time for the quantity to double (when k > 0). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Half-life"?

options.text: The set of input values that make physical sense in the context; endpoints may produce absolute extrema
Local min: concave up valley (f'' > 0); local max: concave down hill (f'' < 0)
To find absolute extrema on [a,b]: 1) find critical points, 2) evaluate f at critical points and endpoints, 3) compare values
t₁/₂ = ln(2)/|k|; the time for the quantity to decrease to half its initial value (when k < 0)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Half-life" is defined as: t₁/₂ = ln(2)/|k|; the time for the quantity to decrease to half its initial value (when k < 0). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Continuous compound interest"?

options.text: Left: sample at left endpoint; Right: sample at right endpoint; Midpoint: sample at center of each subinterval
The + C in every indefinite integral accounts for the fact that F(x) + C has the same derivative for any constant C
f⁻¹ reverses f: if f(a) = b, then f⁻¹(b) = a; domain of f⁻¹ = range of f, and vice versa
A = Pe^(rt); the limit of compound interest as compounding frequency → ∞

quiz_questions.explanation: "Continuous compound interest" is defined as: A = Pe^(rt); the limit of compound interest as compounding frequency → ∞. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Relative growth rate"?

options.text: ∫csc²x dx = -cot x + C; ∫sec x tan x dx = sec x + C; ∫csc x cot x dx = -csc x + C
(1/y)(dy/dt) = k; the growth rate as a fraction of the current value; constant for exponential models
Choose h measuring depth down from the surface (h = 0 at surface); or set up y-axis and convert
f'''(x), f⁴(x), etc.; in physics: position → velocity → acceleration → jerk → snap → crackle → pop

quiz_questions.explanation: "Relative growth rate" is defined as: (1/y)(dy/dt) = k; the growth rate as a fraction of the current value; constant for exponential models. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "dy/dt = ky has solution y(t) = y₀eᵏᵗ; k > 0 = growth, k < 0 = decay"?

options.text: Key points to plot
F increasing/decreasing
Linear approximation
Exponential growth/decay DE

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Exponential growth/decay DE".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "t₂ = ln(2)/k; the time for the quantity to double (when k > 0)"?

options.text: Second derivative
Free response strategy
Inflection point at P = K/2

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "t₁/₂ = ln(2)/|k|; the time for the quantity to decrease to half its initial value (when k < 0)"?

options.text: Half-life
When the test fails
Common quotient rule errors
When does the particle change direction?

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A = Pe^(rt); the limit of compound interest as compounding frequency → ∞"?

options.text: Domain restrictions
When the test fails
Infinite limit

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "(1/y)(dy/dt) = k; the growth rate as a fraction of the current value; constant for exponential models"?

options.text: Relative growth rate
Cardiac output
Epsilon (ε)
Calculator vs. non-calculator

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Exponential Growth and Decay is accurate?

options.text: Key properties: ∫ₐᵇ [f ± g]dx = ∫ₐᵇ f dx ± ∫ₐᵇ g dx (linearity); ∫ₐᵇ cf dx = c∫ₐᵇ f dx (constant multiple); ∫ₐᵇ f dx + ∫ᵇᶜ f dx = ∫ₐᶜ f dx (additivity over intervals); ∫ₐᵃ f dx = 0; ∫ₐᵇ f dx = -∫ᵇₐ f dx (reversing limits).
Differential Equation Basics introduces equations involving derivatives.
Solutions are functions y(x) that satisfy the equation.
Exponential Growth and Decay models situations where the rate of change is proportional to the current amount: dy/dt = ky.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Exponential Growth and Decay as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Exponential Growth and Decay?

options.text: The solution is y = y₀eᵏᵗ, where y₀ is the initial value.
A key AP skill: translating between rate graphs, accumulation functions, and verbal descriptions.
If f'(t) is the rate of water flowing into a tank, then ∫₀ᵀ f'(t)dt is the net change in water volume.
Energy conservation connects work to kinetic and potential energy changes.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Exponential Growth and Decay as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Exponential Growth and Decay?

options.text: When k > 0: exponential growth (population, investment); when k < 0: exponential decay (radioactive decay, drug clearance).
Average Value of a Function extends the concept of arithmetic mean to continuous functions.
The sum, difference, product, quotient, and power laws allow limits to be computed piece by piece.
Antiderivatives: Inverse Trigonometric Forms produces inverse trig functions from specific integrand patterns.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Exponential Growth and Decay as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Exponential Growth and Decay?

options.text: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects differentiation and integration: d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t)dt] = f(x).
Second Derivative Test provides a quick classification of critical points using concavity.
The doubling time (growth) is t₂ = ln(2)/k and the half-life (decay) is t₁/₂ = ln(2)/|k|.
These formulas arise from the Pythagorean theorem applied to infinitesimal arc segments: ds = √(dx² + dy²) = √(1 + (dy/dx)²)dx.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Exponential Growth and Decay as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Exponential Growth and Decay?

options.text: Area Between Curves computes the area enclosed between two functions.
After integrating in terms of u, substitute back: u = g(x).
The fundamental pair: d/dx[sin x] = cos x and d/dx[cos x] = -sin x (derived from the limit definition using lim(h→0) sin(h)/h = 1).
Continuous compound interest follows A = Pe^(rt).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Exponential Growth and Decay as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Exponential Growth and Decay?

options.text: Radiocarbon dating: ¹⁴C decays with t₁/₂ ≈ 5,730 years; measuring remaining ¹⁴C in organic material determines its age up to ~50,000 years
Compound interest with variable rates: dA/dt for A = P·e^(∫r(t)dt) requires the chain rule combined with the FTC for variable-rate financial modeling
AP exam problems frequently ask students to apply MVT to piecewise data: 'Given the table, must there be a value c where f'(c) = 3?'
Quality control: using normal distribution integrals to determine the probability that a manufactured part falls within specifications (Six Sigma methodology)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Exponential Growth and Decay.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Exponential Growth and Decay?

options.text: Computing moments of inertia in physics: ∫x²·f(x)dx often requires integration by parts, essential for structural and mechanical engineering
Marathon pace strategy: analyzing a runner's velocity function to identify periods of acceleration (increasing velocity) and deceleration (decreasing velocity)
Orbital mechanics: computing orbital periods and trajectories involves integrals with expressions like √(a²-x²), solved with trigonometric substitution
Investment growth: at 7% continuous compound interest, doubling time = ln(2)/0.07 ≈ 10 years (the 'Rule of 70')

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Exponential Growth and Decay.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Exponential Growth and Decay applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Proving that a polynomial of degree n has at most n-1 critical points: applying Rolle's Theorem between consecutive roots of f
Marathon pace strategy: analyzing a runner's velocity function to identify periods of acceleration (increasing velocity) and deceleration (decreasing velocity)
Bacterial growth: E. coli doubles every 20 minutes in ideal conditions; 1 bacterium becomes ~70 billion in 12 hours (2³⁶ ≈ 6.9 × 10¹⁰)
Total rainfall accumulation: if r(t) is the rainfall rate at time t, then total accumulated rainfall by time x is R(x) = ∫₀ˣ r(t)dt

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Exponential Growth and Decay.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Exponential Growth and Decay?

options.text: Radiocarbon dating: ¹⁴C decays with t₁/₂ ≈ 5,730 years; measuring remaining ¹⁴C in organic material determines its age up to ~50,000 years
Marathon pace strategy: analyzing a runner's velocity function to identify periods of acceleration (increasing velocity) and deceleration (decreasing velocity)
Temperature modeling: since temperature changes continuously, the IVT guarantees that between any two measured temperatures, every intermediate value was achieved
Ladder sliding down a wall: if the base moves away at 2 ft/s, how fast does the top slide down? Classic Pythagorean related rates problem used in construction safety

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Exponential Growth and Decay.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Exponential Growth and Decay in practice?

options.text: Engineering error analysis: propagation of measurement uncertainty through formulas using differentials to estimate how input errors affect computed results
Trajectory optimization: confirming that a critical point of a fuel consumption function represents minimum fuel use (f'' > 0) rather than maximum
Investment growth: at 7% continuous compound interest, doubling time = ln(2)/0.07 ≈ 10 years (the 'Rule of 70')
Engineering: computing cross-sectional area of unusually shaped beams or channels for structural and flow analysis

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Exponential Growth and Decay.
